Natural Disasters as Macro-Liminality In the above model, referring to large-scale liminality in ‘moments’, we also invoked the example of sudden disasters, which can involve a larger population and throw a whole society into ‘forced’ and (initially) non-ritualized liminality. Concerning natural disasters, such as floods or earthquakes, such events will normally come as sudden ruptures and last for brief but dramatic moments. Logically, one would also assume that such disasters almost by definition cannot involve entire civilizations, bound in space and time as they are. However, there are numerous examples where this has indeed been the case, and such moments, even if localized and densely temporalized, can have lasting effects far beyond the given space and time. In fact, Minoan culture, this deep well of Europe’s cultural heritage, developed in close affinity with earthquakes and floods – and it was finally undone by a devastating flood and its consequences. With respect to European modernity, one event immediately presents itself as the perfect candidate for exemplifying large- scale liminality rupturing in a single moment: the Lisbon Earthquake of 1755. Let us briefly consider this event as a liminal moment with larger social consequences, to exemplify the mechanisms at play. The other dimensions of societal large-scale liminality (revolutions, wars, longer periods of war and political unrest) will be taken up explicitly in the chapters that follow. The Lisbon Earthquake of 1755 Oh, miserable mortals! Oh wretched earth! Oh, dreadful assembly of all mankind! Eternal sermon of useless sufferings! Deluded philosophers who cry, ‘All is well’, Hasten, contemplate these frightful ruins, This wreck, these shreds, these wretched ashes of the dead; These women and children heaped on one another, These scattered members under broken marble; Voltaire, Poem of the Lisbon Disaster, or, An Examination of that Axiom, ‘All is Well’ The Lisbon earthquake is without doubt the most dramatic natural disaster in modern European history. It is one of those sudden events that presents itself as a rupture into every level of the taken-for-granted. It occurred, incredibly enough, on November 1, All Saints’ Day, in many ways the most holy of all holy days, at around 09:40 local time, just as masses of people were assembled for prayer in the Dimensions of Liminality 95 city’s churches. Twelve-metre high waves slammed at the city, destabilizing even the major church towers which collapsed and crashed through roofs, crushing the people praying below. The brothels, located further up the hills, were spared – a fact that did not go unnoticed in the subsequent attempts to make sense of the event. Fires broke out at several places, merging into a veritable firestorm. Those who were not killed outright or trapped in the rubble, ran in panic down to the quays along the Tejo River, only to be swept away by one of the largest tsunamis ever generated in the Atlantic Ocean. The artistic presentations of the event leave us no doubt about it: this was experienced as the ultimate cataclysm, the disappearance of everything that one had been holding on to; this was Hell on Earth. It must be remembered that Lisbon was then one of the five largest cities in Europe. As a harbour town and Portugal’s capital, it was an extremely important trade hub, and still one of the most important passage points between Europe and its colonies. Most of its approximately 275,000 inhabitants were devoutly Catholic; the cathedrals and churches in town were filled to the brim when the earth started to tremble. It is still the worst earthquake (technically speaking a seaquake) recorded in Western history. Some claim that one quarter of the city’s inhabitants were wiped out in no time. Numbers are still disputed, but it is a fair estimate that approximately 70,000 people died instantly – an enormity. No-one was prepared for it – and there were no masters of ceremony to hold on to. This was a local event, but it sent shock-waves throughout Europe – and this Tardean metaphor of ‘waves’ becomes relevant here for several reasons. First, it was something literally felt far away from the epicentre of the catastrophe, damaging towns and villages from Morocco to France. Tremors were felt throughout Europe, and there were tidal surges observed as far away as Finland and at the mouth of the Elbe, not far from Kant’s otherwise quite protected Königsberg. Second, as a single rupture, it exerted a huge impact on Europe’s intellectual life. It helped to shape the entire Enlightenment. It was a formative impact on a series of thinkers, including Kant. In contrast to many other previous and later disasters in European history, the Lisbon earthquake was more than a local disaster that changed the life of the Portuguese population, then fading into oblivion (see Larsen 2006). This was a genuine cataclysm, with lasting impacts on European science, social life, religion and philosophy. It was an important event even for the history of city planning, as the reconstruction of the city turned into a full-blown experiment of rationalist urban planning. The city centre was rebuilt with a remarkable rectangular grid (considering the curved hills on which it is built); the admirably broad Avenidas (such as Avenida da Libertade) are reminiscent of the Paris boulevards. In the urban rationalization process, religious forces were stripped of their powers (and property) to vie for secular needs; at a very direct level, the event sparked the twin modern processes of rationalization and secularization (although the Church would later regain much of its property and power). Conversely, the event strengthened Rousseau in his anti-urban thinking (as he wrote in his August 18, 1756 letter to Liminality and the Modern 96 Voltaire, angrily reacting against his poem on the earthquake, ‘If the residents of this large city had been more evenly dispersed and less
